questions.scenario:
The pull request says: add the first private invoice bucket for `devpolaris-orders` in production. The plan summary says:

```text
Terraform will perform the following actions:

  # aws_s3_bucket.orders_invoices will be created
  + resource "aws_s3_bucket" "orders_invoices" {
      + bucket = "dp-orders-invoices-prod"
      + tags   = {
          + "environment" = "prod"
          + "owner"       = "platform"
          + "service"     = "orders-api"
        }
    }

Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.
```

What is the best review comment?

options.text: The plan must be rejected because every first Terraform run should show at least one data source read.
The plan is unsafe because any line starting with `+` means Terraform will destroy something first.
The plan broadly matches the request because it creates one expected bucket and shows no updates or destroys.
The plan is complete proof that the cloud account will accept the bucket name and all future settings.

questions.explanation: The plan action and summary match the stated intent: one bucket is being created, with no changes or destroys. A reviewer should still check whether the settings are complete enough, but the plan itself does not show a surprise. A `+` means create, not destroy, and a plan is not a guarantee that every provider API call will succeed. See [Terraform plan command](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cli/commands/plan).

options.text: Stop and ask why a tag-only request is replacing the bucket and changing its real name.
Approve because `-/+` is Terraform's symbol for a harmless metadata refresh.
Ignore the destroy count because it is balanced by one add.
Approve because adding one tag always requires replacement in Terraform.

questions.explanation: The plan does not match a tag-only intent. `-/+` and the destroy count mean replacement, and the bucket name is changing. A balanced add and destroy can still be risky because it may replace a real object the team meant to keep.

questions.scenario: A pull request changes the production invoice bucket, and its saved Terraform plan is the main approval evidence before apply. Which two checks directly establish that the plan matches the requested lifecycle change?

options.text: Read the affected resource addresses and important attribute changes, including any replacement markers.
Approve from the summary if additions outnumber destroys, because net resource count captures lifecycle risk.
Compare the action summary with the story in the pull request.
Regenerate the plan with refresh disabled so outside changes cannot complicate the requested review.

questions.explanation: A useful review compares the pull request story with concrete addresses, action symbols, and important attribute changes. Net counts cannot show which object is destroyed or replaced. Disabling refresh can hide real drift and makes the approval evidence less complete rather than easier to trust.

options.text: Request clarification or a fix because the destroy is outside the stated intent.
Approve because the create action is present and that was the main request.
Approve because Terraform always deletes temporary buckets during a first bucket change.
Ignore the destroy because it appears after the create in the output.

questions.explanation: The plan contains an extra lifecycle change that the request did not explain. A destroy can be correct only when the team intends and reviews it. Output order does not make the operation safer.

questions.scenario: The orders team has reviewed a plan for its first production invoice bucket. During the change window, the operator runs `terraform apply`; Terraform prints the actions again and waits for approval before calling AWS. The team requires a final human checkpoint for production writes. What purpose does this pause serve?

options.text: Apply always asks because `terraform validate` has not run yet.
Apply is about to change real infrastructure, so the operator gets one more chance to confirm the plan still matches intent.
The prompt is only a logging feature and has no relationship to risk.
Apply cannot read the Terraform files until a person types approval.

questions.explanation: The approval prompt exists because apply changes real resources. It is the last review checkpoint before Terraform calls provider APIs. Validation may already have run, and the prompt is not just logging. See [Terraform apply command](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cli/commands/apply).

questions.scenario: Yesterday the orders team approved a saved plan targeting `eu-west-2`. Before today's production apply, a commit changes the provider region to `eu-west-1`, but the resource block names remain the same. The team requires approval evidence to match the exact code and provider target being applied. What must happen next?

options.text: Apply with auto approval because provider region changes are invisible to resources.
Skip Terraform and create the bucket by hand in both regions.
Apply the old approval because the resource block name did not change.
Run a fresh plan and review it again before applying.

questions.explanation: The approved evidence is no longer current after the provider target changed. A fresh plan is needed because the same resource block can point at a different region or account. Manual creation would break the reviewable Terraform workflow.

questions.scenario: Terraform reports `Apply complete! Resources: 1 added, 0 changed, 0 destroyed.` for the new invoice bucket. The release requirement includes confirming the real bucket exists with the expected region, tags, and access controls before the ticket closes. What should the team do next?

options.text: Run apply repeatedly until the output changes to one destroyed resource.
Assume no verification is needed because successful apply means every operational setting is perfect.
Delete the Terraform files because the bucket now exists.
Verify the real infrastructure or trusted inventory so the team knows the intended object exists with the expected settings.

questions.explanation: A successful apply is important evidence, but teams still verify the real system or an approved inventory view. Terraform files remain the source of desired state. Repeated applies should converge toward no changes, not destruction.
